content.full_text:
P7100

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

May 18, 1977

77-05-245

Mr. Melvin Perlitsch
2344 North Catalina
Burbank, California 91505

Dear Mr. Perlitsch:

I am responding to your unaddressed letter of April 29,
1977, in which you ask whether a city councilman who is a
member of a redevelopment agency board of directors may vote
on a matter having an effect upon the interest of a former
campaign contributor.

The answer is yes. There is nothing in your letter of
April 29 to indicate that a conflict of interest prohibited
by Government Code §87100 would result from such a trans-
action.

87100. No public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which
he knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

87103. An official has a financial interest in
a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the
regular course of business, aggregating two

Melvin Perlitsch
May 18, 1977
                                                        Page Two

     hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
received by or promised to the public official
within twelve months prior to the time when
the decision is made; or

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management. For purposes of this section,
indirect investment or interest means any
investment or interest owned by the spouse
or dependent child of a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a public official,
by any business entity controlled by the
public official or by a trust in which he
has a substantial interest. A business
entity is controlled by a public official
if the public official, his agents, spouse
and dependent children hold more than fifty
percent of the ownership interest in the
entity. A public official has a substan-
tial interest in a trust when the official,
his spouse and dependent children have a
present or future interest worth more than
one thousand dollars ($1,000).


     Your letter indicates neither that the contributor is a
source of income to the councilman (see exceptions to income,
Government Code §82030(b)), nor that the decision will have
a material financial effect upon a source of income to the
councilman. Thus, based upon all that you have indicated in
your letter, Mr. Gorlick appears correct in his assessment
of the councilman's conflict responsibility.

     This letter does not constitute advice pursuant to Govern-
ment Code §83114(b).

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division


DLS:glb

cc:  Samuel Gorlick

May 5 [illegible]

29 April 1977

Please tell me whether your office sees the following as a conflict of interest:

Two years ago, incumbent Burbank City Councilman Leland Ayers was a candidate for Supervisor in the 5th Supervisorial District of Los Angeles County. One of his large contributors for that campaign was actress Debby Reynolds, I am told. He has not denied that this allegation is true.

On Tuesday April 26, 1977, Councilman Ayers refused to disqualify himself from voting on the matter of appropriating $10,000 of Burbank Development Agency funds (Mr. Ayers is a Director of that Agency in addition to being a Burbank City Councilman) to pay for housing Debby Reynolds's collection of memorabilia which is destined to be placed in a museum which will also be financed by that same Agency.

When Mr. Ayers was challenged on his refusal to disqualify himself at the said Council meeting, City Attorney Samuel Gorlick stated that no conflict of interest existed and Mr. Ayers should be able to vote. Mr. Ayers voted and the appropriation was approved.

Your reply will be appreciated.

Sincerely,

[Signature]
Melvin Perlich
Melvin Perlich
2441 North Ontario
Burbank CA 91505

sections.question: Please tell me whether your office sees the following as a conflict of interest: Two years ago, incumbent Burbank City Councilman Beland Ayers was a candidate for Supervisor in the Fifth Supervisorial District of Los Angeles County. One of his large contributors for that campaign was actress Debby Reynolds, I am told. He has not denied that this allegation is true. On Tuesday April 26, 1977, Councilman Ayers refused to disqualify himself from voting on the matter of appropriating $10,000 of Burbank Redevelopment Agency funds (Mr. Ayers is a Director of that Agency in addition to being a Burbank City Councilman) to pay for housing Debby Reynold's collection of memorabilia which is destined to be placed in a museum which will also be financed by that same Agency. When Mr. Ayers was challenged on his refusal to disqualify himself at the said Council meeting, City Attorney Samuel Gorlick stated that no conflict of interest existed and Mr. Ayers should be able to vote. Mr. Ayers voted and the appropriation was approved.
sections.conclusion: The answer is yes. There is nothing in your letter of April 29 to indicate that a conflict of interest prohibited by Government Code §87100 would result from such a transaction. Your letter indicates neither that the contributor is a source of income to the councilman (see exceptions to income, Government Code §82030(b)), nor that the decision will have a material financial effect upon a source of income to the councilman. Thus, based upon all that you have indicated in your letter, Mr. Gorlick appears correct in his assessment of the councilman's conflict responsibility.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilman who is also a member of a redevelopment agency board may vote on a matter affecting the interests of a former campaign contributor without creating a conflict of interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that no conflict of interest exists under Government Code §87100 because the letter provides no evidence that the former contributor is a source of ongoing income to the councilman or that the decision would have a material financial effect on the councilman's income. The decision does not meet the statutory definition of a financial interest requiring recusal.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the original incoming request letter (dated April 29, 1977) and the FPPC response letter (dated May 18, 1977). The response clearly identifies the legal question and provides a definitive conclusion with statutory citations. Letter explicitly states it does not constitute formal advice under Government Code §83114(b).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Please tell me whether your office sees the following as a conflict of interest: Two years ago, incumbent Burbank City Councilman Beland Ayers was a candidate for Supervisor in the Fifth Supervisorial District of Los Angeles County. One of his large contributors for that campaign was actress Debby Reynolds, I am told. He has not denied that this allegation is true. On Tuesday April 26, 1977, Councilman Ayers refused to disqualify himself from voting on the matter of appropriating $10,000 of Burbank Redevelopment Agency funds (Mr. Ayers is a Director of that Agency in addition to being a Burbank City Councilman) to pay for housing Debby Reynold's collection of memorabilia which is destined to be placed in a museum which will also be financed by that same Agency. When Mr. Ayers was challenged on his refusal to disqualify himself at the said Council meeting, City Attorney Samuel Gorlick stated that no conflict of interest existed and Mr. Ayers should be able to vote. Mr. Ayers voted and the appropriation was approved.

CONCLUSION: The answer is yes. There is nothing in your letter of April 29 to indicate that a conflict of interest prohibited by Government Code §87100 would result from such a transaction. Your letter indicates neither that the contributor is a source of income to the councilman (see exceptions to income, Government Code §82030(b)), nor that the decision will have a material financial effect upon a source of income to the councilman. Thus, based upon all that you have indicated in your letter, Mr. Gorlick appears correct in his assessment of the councilman's conflict responsibility.
embedding.first_500_words: May 18, 1977 Mr. Melvin Perlitsch 2344 North Catalina Burbank, California 91505 Dear Mr. Perlitsch: I am responding to your unaddressed letter of April 29, 1977, in which you ask whether a city councilman who is a member of a redevelopment agency board of directors may vote on a matter having an effect upon the interest of a former campaign contributor. The answer is yes. There is nothing in your letter of April 29 to indicate that a conflict of interest prohibited by Government Code §87100 would result from such a transaction. 87100. No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. 87103. An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); (b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made; or (d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, by any business entity controlled by the public official or by a trust in which he has a substantial interest. A business entity is controlled by a public official if the public official, his agents, spouse and dependent children hold more than fifty percent of the ownership interest in the entity. A public official has a substantial interest in a trust when the official, his spouse and dependent children have a present or future interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). Your letter indicates neither that the contributor is a source of income to the councilman (see exceptions to income, Government Code §82030(b)), nor that the decision will have a material financial effect upon a source of income to the councilman. Thus, based upon all that you have indicated in your letter, Mr. Gorlick appears correct in his assessment of the councilman's conflict responsibility. This letter does not constitute advice pursuant to Government Code §83114(b). Sincerely, Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief Conflicts of Interest Division DLS:glb cc: Samuel Gorlick 29 April 1977
embedding.summary: FPPC Chief Delbert Spurlock responds to an inquiry about whether a Burbank City Councilman's vote on a redevelopment agency appropriation benefiting a former campaign contributor constitutes a conflict of interest. The FPPC concludes no conflict exists under Government Code §87100, as the letter does not establish that the contributor is a source of income or that the decision would materially affect the councilman's financial interests.
